summary: Martin reflects on programmers' dual responsibility by juxtaposing two events: SpaceX's Falcon Heavy launch that sent a Tesla to the asteroid belt, and the death of Elaine Herzberg, killed by an Uber self-driving car. He argues that programmers enabled both the triumph and the tragedy through their code. The post challenges the notion that ethics should be separate from programming practices, asserting that the profession's impact on society makes moral considerations intrinsic to the work. Martin concludes that programmers can no longer isolate themselves in 'techie bubbles' and must recognize that every line of code carries ethical weight.
key_points: Software permeates every aspect of modern achievements - from CAD/CAM to simulations to communications, billions of lines of code enabled SpaceX's historic launch
Programmers bear collective responsibility for harms caused by their code, even when no individual acted maliciously or negligently
Machine learning systems are 'insidiously difficult to understand,' making it potentially impossible to identify why autonomous systems fail
The sentiment that ethics should be excluded from discussions of programming practices is disturbing given software's life-and-death consequences
Code now affects everything from baby monitors to nuclear weapons policy, making programmer isolation from societal concerns untenable
money_quotes: Every minute step along the pathway from the dream, to the realization, was lubricated, enabled, enhanced, and simplified by software.
It was a message that we sent to ourselves, and to the universe at large, saying that we are coming, and nothing in this universe will stop us.
Elaine Herzberg is dead. She was struck by a 'self-driving' car while walking her bicycle across the road. And we programmers killed her.
Perhaps there was an IF statement somewhere in that code that, had the boolean predicate been in the opposite state, would have prevented the collision.
Machine learning neural networks are insidiously difficult to understand.
It seems to me that ethics and morality have become intrinsic to everything we programmers do; because so very much depends upon the quality of our work.
We programmers must no longer hide in our little techie bubbles.
Every line of code represents an ethical and moral decision.
key_insight: Programmers can no longer treat their work as purely technical because the ubiquity of software in modern life makes every line of code an ethical and moral decision with real consequences for human lives.
